coredata.dc:title: Improving the biocompatibility of carbon nanodots for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Quan-Xing
Han, Lu
Shu, Yang
Chen, Xu-Wei
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: In the practice of in vivo imaging with carbon nanodots (CNDs) as probe, the volume of CNDs solution introduced into living body should be kept at minimum, and a higher concentration is needed to ensure sufficient quantity of the probe for obtaining bright image. Therefore, the improvement on biocompatibility of the CNDs is among the most important and critical issues. We report herein the improvement on the biocompatibility of CNDs with modification by ionic liquid. Amide group functionalization of carbon nanodots is first conducted through microwave irradiation, followed by coupling the ionic liquid 1-carboxymethyl-3-methyl imidazolium bromide on the surface of the Amide-CNDs via covalent conjunction to produce the modified carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs). This modification process significantly improved the biocompatibility of CNDs, as demonstrated by cell imaging at a higher concentration of CNDs. Both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs exhibit abundant surface functional groups, resulting in tunable fluorescent emission feature and potential applications in two-color cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Biocompatibility
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots
Preparation and characterization of the ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21235010, 21275027 , 21475017 and 21575020 ), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N130105002, N152004005 and N150502001 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra (A) and surface charge analysis results (B) of the amide groups functionalized carbon nanodots (Amide-CNDs) and ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs).
HRTEM images of amide groups functionalized carbon nanodots (Amide-CNDs) and ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs). The insets illustrate their size distribution histograms.

The photoluminescence spectra of Amide-CNDs (A) and IL-CNDs (B); the variation of photoluminescence with pH (C); UV–vis absorption spectra of Amide CNDs and IL-CNDs (D). Both concentrations of Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs are fixed at 1.0mgmL−1.
HeLa cell viability by incubating with Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs at various concentration levels.

(A) The images of HeLa cells after incubation for 12h with Amide-CNDs at concentrations of 0.5mgmL−1 (i, iv), 1mgmL−1 (ii, v) and 2mgmL−1 (iii, vi); (B) The images of HeLa cells after incubation for 12h with IL-CND at concentrations of 1mgmL−1 (i, iv), 2mgmL−1 (ii, v) and 4mgmL−1 (iii, vi). Taken at bright field (i, ii, iii) and with excitation/emission wavelengths at 330–380/420nm (iv, v, vi).
The images of HeLa cells after incubation for 12h with 0.5mgmL−1 of Amide-CNDs (A) and 2.0mgmL−1 IL-CNDs CDs (B). Taken at a bright field (i); with excitation at 330–380nm (ii) and 450–490nm (iii).
Schematic illustration for the preparation of IL-CNDs.

head.ce:title: Improving the biocompatibility of carbon nanodots for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biotechnology, College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110169, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biotechnology, College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the practice of in vivo imaging with carbon nanodots (CNDs) as probe, the volume of CNDs solution introduced into living body should be kept at minimum, and a higher concentration is needed to ensure sufficient quantity of the probe for obtaining bright image. Therefore, the improvement on biocompatibility of the CNDs is among the most important and critical issues. We report herein the improvement on the biocompatibility of CNDs with modification by ionic liquid. Amide group functionalization of carbon nanodots is first conducted through microwave irradiation, followed by coupling the ionic liquid 1-carboxymethyl-3-methyl imidazolium bromide on the surface of the Amide-CNDs via covalent conjunction to produce the modified carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs). This modification process significantly improved the biocompatibility of CNDs, as demonstrated by cell imaging at a higher concentration of CNDs. Both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs exhibit abundant surface functional groups, resulting in tunable fluorescent emission feature and potential applications in two-color cell imaging.

Ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots are prepared via covalent conjugation between carboxyl and amide groups functionalized carbon nanodots which exhibit significant improvement on the biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-toxic and non-bleaching fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) have attracted widespread attention as an alternative to semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes [1]. CNDs with sizes below 10nm have become an important member of carbon nanomaterials since its first discovery in 2004 [2,3]. Due to the high solubility, facile modification feature, and special fluorescent property, CNDs show high potentials in biomedical, optronic and catalytic applications as well as biosensing and bioimaging investigations [4–18]. A notable feature of CNDs is the excitation-dependent fluorescent emission, and the tunable fluorescent property related to surface functionalization groups [19,20].

CNDs are usually prepared via either top-down or bottom-up approaches [21]. Element doping and surface modifications are usually employed to improve the fluorescent property. CNDs doped with metals, e.g., zinc, and nonmetals, e.g., boron, nitrogen, sulfur and phosphorus, exhibit enhancement on the quantum yield or shift on the maximum excitation/emission wavelength [22–26]. The wavelength of emission light varied with surface groups which introduce different trapping states with various energy levels. As illustrated by the red-shift fluorescent emission of CNDs with higher surface oxidation and the blue-shift of fluorescent emission after reduction [20,27]. For this purpose, polyethylenimine (PEI) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) or their derivatives are frequently used as surface modification agents for nanomaterials. It has been reported that CNDs passivated with poly(propionylethylenimine -co-ethylenimine) (PPEI-EI) and PEG exhibit obvious improvement on the solubility as well as fluorescence [28], and a red shift on the fluorescent emission of CNDs is observed after modification with PEI [29]. A simple one-pot procedure is reported with PEI as both carbon source and passivation agent for the production of pH sensitive CNDs [30]. There are also reports on the application of PEI functionalized CNDs for bio-imaging and nucleic acid delivery, although the cell cytotoxicity of PEI-CNDs is higher than that of CNDs functionalized with other surface groups, e.g., PEG or carboxylic groups [31–33].

Ionic liquids (IL) are generally organic salts consist of cationic and anionic moieties which provide flexible alternatives for the regulation of their physicochemical properties. The unique features of ionic liquids, e.g., negligible vapor pressure and high chemical/thermal stability, endow them favorable biocompatibility and make them highly suitable for biological related studies, e.g., extraction/isolation of macrobiomolecules [34,35] and biocatalysis [36]. Recently, ionic liquid has been used for the functionalization of CNDs via electrochemical exfoliation of graphite, which find applications in electrocatalysis, biosensing and bioimaging [37,38]. Ionic liquid modified CNDs obtained via hydrothermal treatment show tunable amphiphilicity and multi-functional applications, e.g., ion sensing and fluorescent ink [39].

In the present study, an ambient-temperature approach is reported for the functionalization of carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs) by using ionic liquid 1-carboxymethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (CmimCl), through covalent coupling between the carboxyl ionic liquid and the amide groups functionalized carbon nanodots (Amide-CNDs). The obtained IL-CNDs exhibit obvious change on the fluorescent emission feature and their biocompatibility has been significantly enhanced, which facilitate bioimaging applications.

D(+)-Glucosamide hydrochloride, branched polyethylenimine (B-PEI) are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, USA). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and 2,4,6-trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid are obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ionic liquid used in the present study, e.g., 1-carboxymethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (CmimCl), is the product of Cheng Jie Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Diethylenetriamine (DTM), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), HCl, NaOH, NaCl, KCl, Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 are obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), trypsin (0.25%), penicillin, streptomycin are purchased from Hyclone (Logan, USA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit is obtained from Nanjing KeyGEN Biotech Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All the reagents used are at least of analytical reagent grade unless otherwise specified. Deionized water (DI water) of 18MΩcm is used throughout.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Amide functionalized carbon nanodots (Amide-CNDs) are prepared firstly via a microwave procedure with glucosamine as a carbon source, B-PEI and DTM serve as passivation agents. Typically, 5mL glucosamine solution (0.2gmL−1), 2mL B-PEI solution (0.25gmL−1), 400μL DTM and 8mL pure water are mixed and sealed into a 100-mL Teflon digestion tank and heated at 200°C for 15min in a COOLPEX Microwave Chemical Reaction Apparatus (PreeKem Scientific Instruments Co. Ltd., China). After cooling down to ambient temperature, the solution is then dialyzed over deionized water in a dialysis bag (MWCO membranes 3500Da) for 48h. The solution in the dialysis bag is then lyophilized. The obtained product is dispersed in pure water at a concentration of 15.3mgmL−1.

Ionic liquids modified carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs) are prepared via covalent bonding interaction. Typically, 153mg CmimCl, 830mg EDC·HCl, and 1.24g NHS are added into 10mL PBS buffer solution (10mM, pH 5.5). The mixture is magnetically stirred for 15min. Subsequently, 1mL Amide-CNDs solution (15.3mgmL−1) is added. Afterwards, pH value of the mixture is adjusted to 7.4 with sodium hydroxide, and the reaction is allowed to continue for 12h under stirring. The solution is then dialyzed against water in a dialysis bag (MWCO membranes 1000Da) for 72h followed by lyophilization. It is dispersed in water and the concentration of the obtained product is derived to be 100mgmL−1. This process is illustrated in Scheme 1.

A PB-10 pH meter (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) is used to measure the pH values. UV–vis spectra are recorded on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). Photoluminescence behavior is evaluated by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) and quantum yields are quantified with a Fluormax 4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Scientific, France). Surface charge analysis for the carbon nanodots is conducted by using a nano ZS-90 particle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments, United Kingdom). A Nicolet-6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) is employed to record the FT-IR spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are recorded on a MPDDY2094 X-ray diffractometer (PAnalytical B.V, Netherlands) with Cu-Ka irradiation (λ=1.5406Å) in the range of 2θ from 10 to 80°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are obtained using a JEM-2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). HeLa cells are cultured in a HERA Cell 150 incubator (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA), and their images are obtained on an inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon, Japan). MTT assay is conducted by using a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader at 490nm (BioTek, USA).

HeLa cells are cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% (v/v) penicillin/streptomycin at 37°C in a humidified environment of 5% CO2. In a typical culture procedure, HeLa cells are first inoculated in T-flasks with a culture base area of 25cm2 and cultured for 48h. They are digested with 1mL trypsin (0.25%) solution for 1min, and then centrifuged at 800rpm for 2min. The supernatant is replaced by 1mL PBS solution, followed by HeLa cell counting with a blood counting chamber. Usually, cell density is in the range of 2–3×106/mL at the present culture conditions. Afterwards, the PBS suspension of HeLa cells are centrifuged at 800rpm for 2min, and the supernatant is replaced by 1mL DMEM medium supplemented with FBS and penicillin/streptomycin.

MTT is employed to test the cytotoxicity of carbon nanodots as illustrated in our previous report [40]. In a typical MTT assay, the DMEM suspension of HeLa cells is diluted using DMEM medium supplemented with FBS and penicillin/streptomycin, and 100μL of which (containing ca. 7000–10000 cells) is added to each well of a 96-well plate. The cells are cultured for 12h, and the culture medium is then replaced by fresh portion containing CNDs of various concentrations. After further culture for 20h, 20μL of MTT solution (5mgmL−1) is added into each well, followed by culture for 4h. The culture medium is then replaced by 150μL DMSO. After mechanical shaking for 5min in the Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader, the absorbance (A) at 490nm of the mixture is recorded, and the cell viability is derived.

For imaging studies, the HeLa cells are inoculated in glass T-flasks and cultured for 24h, and the culture medium is then replaced with new portion containing various concentrations of Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs. After incubation for 12h, the culture medium is removed and the cells are washed thoroughly with phosphate buffer (PBS, 10mmolL−1, pH 7.4). The HeLa cell imaging is finally recorded by using an inverted fluorescence microscope with excitation/emission wavelength at 330–380/420nm and 450–490/520nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the ionic liquid modified carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra of the prepared Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs are illustrated in Fig. 1(A). It is seen that the absorption bands for primary amide at 3382cm−1, 3270cm−1 (stretching vibration) and 1672cm−1 (bending vibration) are identified in the spectrum of Amide-CNDs. On the other hand, the spectrum of IL-CNDs is characterized by the absorption bands for amideⅠat 1641cm−1 (stretching vibration of C˭O) and amide Ⅱat 1572cm−1 (bending vibration of C-N-H). In the present study, ionic liquid 1-carboxymethyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride is immobilized on the surface of Amide-CNDs via covalent bonding to produce IL-CNDs, and FT-IR spectra identified clearly the transformation of surface functional groups of the CNDs. This transformation is further confirmed by XPS spectra illustrated in Fig. S1, indicating that both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs mainly contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. S1A). High resolution spectra of the N1S peaks at 398.5eV for Amide-CNDs and 398.9eV for IL-CNDs are attributed to C–N–C bond that may come from PEI or DTM [39]. While the peaks at 400.3eV for Amide-CNDs and 400.7eV for IL-CNDs are attributed to N-(C)3 bond that may come from imidazolium group [39]. It is obvious that the relative intensity of C–N–C bond decreases and the relative intensity of N-(C)3 bond increases (Fig. S1B). This change is attributed to the introduction of ionic liquid. Besides, the atomic contents of Amide-CNDs are derived to be 68.67% for C, 17.41% for N, 13.92% for O, while for IL-CNDs, the contents are 62.58% for C, 11.58 for N, 24.51 for O, and 1.33% for Cl.

The surface charge analysis results for the Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs as illustrated in Fig. 1(B) further confirm the change of surface functional groups. It is noteworthy that the isoelectric point of Amide-CNDs is in the range of pH 11–12 due to the presence of amide groups, while that of IL-CNDs is transformed to pH 6–7 because of the coverage of CNDs surface by ionic liquid CmimCl. In addition, the surface density of primary amide groups is derived to be 0.13mmolmg−1 for Amide-CNDs and 0.02mmolmg−1 for IL-CNDs via the chromogenic reaction between primary amide and 2,4,6-trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid [41]. This observation indicates that the surface of IL-CNDs is mainly surrounded by ionic liquid moiety in addition to some residual amide groups.

The high resolution TEM images for the prepared carbon nanodots are illustrated in Fig. 2. It is seen clearly that both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs shown uniform morphology with size distributions of 5.25±1.37nm and 5.52±1.15nm, respectively. No lattice diffraction of graphitic structure is observed from the HRTEM images for either Amide-CNDs or IL-CNDs, indicating their amorphous nature as further demonstrated by their XRD patterns given in Fig. S2.

The optical properties of the as-prepared CNDs are illustrated in Fig. 3. It is seen from Fig. 3A and B that both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission behavior, with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 340/460nm for Amide-CNDs and 330/430nm for IL-CNDs. It is noteworthy that the photoluminescent emission of Amide-CNDs is excitation-independent within 300–360nm, while it is excitation-dependent within 380–480nm (Fig. S3A). That is to say the emission wavelength keeps constant at 460nm within an excitation wavelength range of 300–360nm, while it shifts from 468nm to 530nm by excitation within 380nm to 480nm. On the contrary, the photoluminescent emission of IL-CNDs within 433–507nm is excitation-dependent within the whole range of excitation wavelength investigated, i.e., 300–450nm, as clearly indicated in Fig. S3B. The difference in photoluminescence behavior between Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs might be attributed to the surface functional groups, due to the fact that surface state in CNDs is regarded as a vital factor for controlling their photoluminescence features [42,43]. It has been demonstrated that the photoluminescence of CNDs is induced by energy trapping due to the defect sites on the surface of CNDs [44], and different surface functional groups tend to introduce various defect sites which result in multivariate photoluminescence properties [20].

The quantum yield (Ф) of the as-prepared CNDs is calculated by comparing with the integrated photoluminescence and absorbance values with that of quinine sulfate (Table S1). They are derived to be 8.9% and 7.1% for Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs, respectively, which are comparable with that of reported CNDs (Table S2). As discussed in the previous section, the Amide-CNDs are functionalized by amide groups, while the IL-CNDs are mainly decorated by ionic liquid moiety along with some residual amide groups. This induces their distinct photoluminescence properties. Considering that both the amide groups and ionic liquid moiety is proton sensitive, the variation of photoluminescence with pH is further investigated as shown in Fig. 3C. It is seen that photoluminescence intensity of both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs decrease gradually with the increase of pH value from 1.4 to 13. This pH-sensitive feature might find potential applications in proton sensitive processes, e.g., biological metabolization or membrane analysis [9].

UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3D) show an absorption at 272nm and a broad band at ca. 350nm for Amide-CNDs that are attributed to π-π transitions [45]. For the case of IL-CNDs, the π-π transition absorption is blue-shifted to 258nm [45]. The variation of UV–vis absorption reflects the difference of energy levels between Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs, which are closely related to their surface functionalization groups [44].

For the application in bio-imaging, anti-cancer drug delivery or other biological investigations, cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the CNDs are among the most important factors for consideration. It is known that CNDs generally pose minimum cytotoxicity on cells when very low concentration level is employed, with respect to CdSe-based QDs [1,3,46]. It is also have been demonstrated that the cytotoxicity of CNDs is closely related with surface groups and PEI modified CNDs show really high cytotoxicity which may generate cell viability lower than 50% at a low concentration of 0.015mgmL−1 [33,47]. For the present case, the cytotoxicity as investigated via MTT assay ( Fig. 4) indicated that no obvious variation of the cell viability is observed at a very low concentration of <1mgmL−1 for both Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs. At a concentration level of 1mgmL−1 Amide-CNDs, an obvious decrease of cell viability to ca. 80% is encountered. When further increasing the concentration of Amide-CNDs to 4mgmL−1, the cell viability significantly dropped to ca. 50%. On the other hand, however, when incubating with IL-CNDs, the viability of HeLa cell remains virtually unchanged at ca. 100% at a high concentration of IL-CNDs of 6mgmL−1 (Fig. 4). At an even high concentration of 10mgmL−1 IL-CNDs, the cell viability still remains high at ca. 75%. These observations clearly show a much improved biocompatibility by the modification of carbon nanodots with ionic liquid moiety.

The significant difference of cell cytotoxicity between Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs is mainly attributed to the variation of surface functional groups. Amide-CNDs are functionalized by amide groups entailing the surface abundant positive charges at physiological environment, meanwhile the cell membrane is negatively charged as demonstrated in Fig. S4. The electrostatic interaction between Amide-CNDs and cell membrane causes damage on the cell membrane [48], as illustrated in Fig. 5A. By increasing the concentration of Amide-CNDs from 0.5mgmL−1 to 2mgmL−1, obvious damages on the cells and tremendous deformation of cell morphology are observed. On the contrary, the incorporation of ionic liquid moiety onto the CNDs is expected to provide better biocompatibility and facilitate biological applications [49,50]. It is observed that Hela cells incubated with IL-CNDs at concentrations of 1mgmL−1, 2mgmL−1 and 4mgmL−1 keep original morphology and conformational structure (Fig. 5B). This well demonstrated the favorable biocompatibility of ionic liquid moiety modified carbon nanodots (IL-CNDs).

Fig. 5 further illustrated that with the increase of Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs concentration within a certain range (<2mgmL−1 Amide-CNDs and <4mgmL−1 IL-CNDs), the blue fluorescent images of HeLa cells become brighter, as demonstrated by the increase of mean optical density measured using the widely used bio-analysis software ImageJ (Fig. S5) [51,52]. However, for the Hela cells incubated with Amide-CNDs at a higher concentration, the deformation of cell structure induces too vague fluorescent images to distinguish the cell nucleus and cytoplasm. This makes it unfeasible for the acquisition of detailed and distinct analytical signals or information in the cells. On the contrary, however, when incubating with IL-CNDs at an identical concentration and same experimental conditions, the Hela cells are obviously brightened with clear cell contour/morphology and complete cell framework (Fig. 5B). This clearly demonstrated the improvement on biocompatibility of CNDs by decoration with ionic liquid moiety. It should be noted that in practical applications for cell imaging and labeling, high-quality images with bright fluorescence and clear cell contour are urgently required, wherein a larger dosage or a higher concentration of CNDs is generally necessary [53,54]. For in vivo imaging, the volume of CNDs solution introduced into living body should be kept to a minimum, and thus a higher concentration is needed to ensure sufficient quantity of the probe for obtaining bright images [55]. In this respect, the improvement on biocompatibility of the CNDs is among the most important and critical issues to ensure successful biological applications.

The potential of the prepared CNDs as fluorescence probe in bio-imaging is demonstrated by labeling HeLa cells. Their excitation-dependent photoluminescence feature provides alternatives for multi-color imaging. Considering the favorable biocompatibility of the IL-CNDs, a larger amount can be used for cell culture. After incubation with Amide-CNDs (0.5mgmL−1) and IL-CNDs (2mgmL−1) for 12h, the images of HeLa cells are recorded by using an inverted fluorescence microscope ( Fig. 6). It is clearly seen that blue (λem 420nm) and green (λem 520nm) fluorescence cell images are observed by excitation at 330–380nm and 450–490nm, respectively. The results demonstrated that both the Amide-CNDs and IL-CNDs can be used as fluorescent probe for the labeling of cells, and IL-CNDs exhibit much better biocompatibility.

Ionic liquids modified carbon nanodots are obtained via a facile and ambient temperature approach. The modification of ionic liquid moiety onto the CNDs significantly improves their biocompatibility and induces different fluorescent emission features when compared with amide groups modified CNDs. The excellent biocompatibility of the ionic liquid modified CNDs makes them highly suitable for bio-imaging investigations. In addition, the tunable fluorescent emission entails multi-color imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21235010, 21275027, 21475017 and 21575020), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N130105002, N152004005 and N150502001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.talanta.2016.08.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Figure S1
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Parallel comparative studies on the toxic effects of unmodified CdTe quantum dots, gold nanoparticles, and carbon nanodots on live cells as well as green gram sprouts
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Carbon-based Quantum dots for fluorescence imaging of cells and tissues
Color-switchable electroluminescence of carbon dot light-emitting diodes
Carbon dot-incorporated PMO nanoparticles as versatile platforms for the design of ratiometric sensors, multichannel traceable drug delivery vehicles, and efficient photocatalysts
Fluorescent carbon nanodots conjugated with folic acid for distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells
A tunable ratiometric pH sensor based on carbon nanodots for the quantitative measurement of the intracellular pH of whole cells
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
An efficient solid-state synthesis of fluorescent surface carboxylated carbon dots derived from C60 as a label-free probe for iron ions in living cells
Development of a carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent Cu2+ probe suitable for living cell imaging
Germanium-doped carbon dots as a new type of fluorescent probe for visualizing the dynamic invasions of mercury(II) ions into cancer cells
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging and biosensing of superoxide anion in live cells
Synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dots with different photoluminescence properties by controlling the surface states
Highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed terbium-doped blue carbon dots
A two-photon “turn-on” fluorescent probe based on carbon nanodots for imaging and selective biosensing of hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues
An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
Observation of pH-, solvent-, spin-, and excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence from carbon nanoparticles
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
Doped carbon nanoparticles as a new platform for highly photoluminescent dots
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from glucose and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic ability
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Facile synthesis of P-doped carbon quantum dots with highly efficient photoluminescence
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Polyethyleneimine modified fluorescent carbon dots and their application in cell labeling
The production of pH-sensitive photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles by the carbonization of polyethylenimine and their use for bioimaging
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Efficient in vitro and in vivo pulmonary delivery of nucleic acid by carbon dot-based nanocarriers
Toxicity of carbon dots - effect of surface functionalization on the cell viability, reactive oxygen species generation and cell cycle
A dual-ionic liquid microemulsion system for the selective isolation of hemoglobin
Selective extraction/isolation of hemoglobin with ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-trimethylsilylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BtmsimPF(6)
One-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoribbons, nanoparticles, and graphene by the exfoliation of graphite in ionic liquids
Ionic liquid-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanodots and their applications in electrocatalysis, biosensing, and cell imaging
Tunable amphiphilicity and multifunctional applications of ionic-liquid-modified carbon quantum dots
The regulation of hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of carbon dots via a one-pot approach
In situ growth of beta-FeOOH nanorods on graphene oxide with ultra-high relaxivity for in vivo magnetic resonance imaging and cancer therapy
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
